# Sonia Citron is scoring, but Washington still has questions to answer

**Persona:** Sibyl Avery  
**Model:** claude-sonnet-4-6  
**Verdict:** NEEDS_REVISION  
**Excerpt:** Four games in, Sonia Citron is scoring — but her efficiency swings and small sample size leave Washington's biggest questions unanswered.

---

Four games into the 2026 WNBA season, the Washington Mystics are 2-2 and still finding their identity under second-year head coach Sydney Johnson. In that brief stretch, guard Sonia Citron has emerged as one of the team's most consistent offensive contributors — and her early-season line raises genuinely interesting questions about how Johnson intends to use her as the schedule stiffens.

The caveat has to come first, because intellectual honesty demands it: four games is a whisper, not a verdict. Patterns that look meaningful right now can dissolve over a full season's worth of possessions. What the data can tell us is how Citron has shown up so far, and where the contours of her role are beginning to take shape.

Her most complete individual performance arrived on May 15 in Indianapolis, when the Mystics edged the Indiana Fever 104-102 in overtime. That game represented Washington at its most resilient — surviving a hostile road environment, extending into an extra period, and finding a way to win when the margin for error disappeared entirely. Citron was central to that effort. She finished with 26 points on 9-of-15 shooting from the field, connecting on 4-of-8 from three-point range and going 4-of-4 from the free-throw line. The efficiency mattered as much as the volume. Hitting 60 percent from the floor and 50 percent from deep in a game decided by two points in overtime is not incidental — it is load-bearing production, the kind that makes overtime winnable.

Compare that to Washington's May 10 home loss to the New York Liberty, 98-93 in overtime, and a different picture emerges. Citron scored 15 points on 5-of-16 shooting, going 2-of-8 from three. That is a 31.3 percent mark from the floor in a game the Mystics ultimately could not close. The Liberty game and the Fever game share a structural similarity — both went to overtime, both were decided on the margins — but Citron's efficiency in those two contests pulled in opposite directions, and so did the outcomes. That correlation is worth monitoring even if, at this sample size, it would be overreach to declare causation.

The road win at Toronto on May 8 — a 68-65 Mystics victory — represented a different offensive context entirely. That final score signals a grind-it-out environment, the kind of low-possession game where shot quality matters more than shot volume. The Mystics found a way to win there, too. Taken together, Washington has shown the capacity to win ugly and the capacity to win in high-tempo overtime situations. Citron's role appears to flex with the game's demands.

What her early numbers don't yet tell us is equally important. Four games cannot answer whether this is her established offensive ceiling, whether she is capable of sustaining this scoring output against increasingly prepared defenses, or how her usage fluctuates when teammates like Kiki Iriafen — now in her second WNBA season and a clear presence in Washington's frontcourt rotation — are also rolling offensively in the same game. Those interactions take time to reveal themselves in data.

What is visible, and worth appreciating, is that Citron has already demonstrated she can be the best player on the floor in a pressure game. The 26-point, overtime road win in Indiana is a legitimate data point, not noise. The Liberty game is a reminder that her efficiency is not automatic, that she can be contained when her shot isn't falling and the team around her needs her to manufacture something different. Both of those things are true at once.

Sydney Johnson has a guard-heavy roster with real depth at the perimeter, from Rori Harmon to Georgia Amoore to Lucy Olsen and others. How Citron carves out a distinct identity within that group — not just as a scorer but as a consistent one — is the season-long story worth tracking. The early pattern is promising. Whether it holds is a question only the next 36 games can answer.

---
